item.title: POINT OF ORIGIN I 100 years on – A symbol of resilience, trust and sacrifice
item.description: STANDING proudly on the waterfront at Wairiki in Taveuni, Meridian Store has become more than just a place of business. For a century, it has served generations of island families, weathered changing times and remained a symbol of resilience built on sacrifice, determination and community trust.
item.content:encoded:
STANDING proudly on the waterfront at Wairiki in Taveuni, Meridian Store has become more than just a place of business.

For a century, it has served generations of island families, weathered changing times and remained a symbol of resilience built on sacrifice, determination and community trust.

As the store marked its 100th anniversary, managing director Champak Lal Dayaram said the occasion was about much more than celebrating a successful business.

“It is about remembering the journey of one man who left everything behind to search for opportunities in a country he knew nothing about,” he said.

Mr Dayaram told guests that the story began in 1885 in Navsari, Gujarat, India, where his grandfather, Dayaram Fakir, was born during British colonial rule.

He said like many Gujarati of the time, Dayaram believed there were better opportunities beyond India and was willing to take enormous risks to secure a better future. After travelling to Zanzibar in East Africa in 1923, Dayaram heard stories from fellow traders about Fiji.

“They spoke about a place where opportunities were beginning to emerge after the indenture system had ended.”

He said those conversations convinced his grandfather to return to India before boarding a ship from Calcutta for Fiji.

The two-month voyage across the ocean was long and difficult.

“There was very little comfort. Passengers travelled under harsh conditions with limited food, but despite all that, he never turned back.”

After arriving in Fiji in 1923, Dayaram settled in Nausori, on Viti Levu where he earned a living as a tailor, travelling from settlement to settlement selling clothing.

Mr Dayaram said his grandfather’s skills quickly became recognised.

“He was even given a block of land in exchange for sewing a suit. That tells you how valuable tailoring was during those days.”

While living in Nausori, Dayaram frequently listened to traders from Labasa, Vanua Levu talking about Taveuni.

Mr Dayaram said they described an island where former indentured labourers had established farms and where Catholic missionaries in Wairiki required traders who could provide groceries, clothing and other essential supplies.

Realising there was an opportunity to serve the growing community, Dayaram decided to move once again.

“He saw opportunity where others only saw isolation.”

In 1926, he arrived in Taveuni and established Dayaram Store, Tailors and Drapers, which later became Meridian Store.

Initially operating under a sublease, he later secured land from Sepo Dalo of the Mataitoga clan in Navarada.

He said his grandfather became the first Gujarati entrepreneur to establish a business on Taveuni. However, life on the island was far from easy.

His wife, Diwari Ben Fakir, remained in India, leaving him to build the business entirely on his own.

“There was no electricity, no piped water and supplies only arrived once a month. Imagine running a business under those conditions.”

Despite the hardships, Dayaram slowly earned the confidence of customers in Wairiki and neighbouring communities.

He said trust became the foundation upon which the business was built.

“People returned because they knew they would be treated fairly.”

Four years later, Dayaram’s only son, Ambaram Dayaram, arrived in Fiji at the age of 12 after another two-month voyage from India.

He quickly learnt tailoring, bookkeeping, customer service and copra trading while working alongside his father.

“By the time he was a teenager, he was already helping to manage many aspects of the business,” Mr Dayaram said.

In 1935, Ambaram returned to India to marry Narbada Ben before returning to Fiji.

World War II soon separated the young family. He said restrictions on sea travel meant Ambaram was unable to reunite with his wife and daughter for almost 10 years.

item.title: WEDDING BELLS | Charming wedding packages
item.description: SET among lush tropical gardens and Fiji’s largest orchid farm, South Sea Orchids in Nasau, Nadi offers a unique outdoor venue for weddings, receptions, engagements, and other special celebrations. Whether you’re planning an intimate ceremony or a larger gathering, their spacious grounds provide a naturally beautiful setting surrounded by orchids, tropical plants, open lawns, and peaceful countrys
item.content:encoded:
SET among lush tropical gardens and Fiji’s largest orchid farm, South Sea Orchids in Nasau, Nadi offers a unique outdoor venue for weddings, receptions, engagements, and other special celebrations.

Whether you’re planning an intimate ceremony or a larger gathering, their spacious grounds provide a naturally beautiful setting surrounded by orchids, tropical plants, open lawns, and peaceful countryside.

Every corner of the farm offers a picturesque backdrop, making it ideal for both your ceremony and wedding photography.

The venue can accommodate everything from small private ceremonies to celebrations of over 200 guests, with plenty of open space to create a layout that suits your wedding style.

Choose from garden ceremonies, outdoor receptions, or a combination of both, all within the one location.

They offer a range of wedding packages designed to suit different styles, guest numbers, and budgets.

If you have something specific in mind, they would be happy to work with you to create a package that fits your vision.

Their catering options are flexible, giving you the freedom to choose a menu that best suits your celebration. Whether you prefer a formal sit-down meal, a buffet, or light refreshments, they’ll work with you to ensure your guests are well looked after even if you prefer to self-cater.

To make planning even easier, they also offer décor hire, allowing you to transform the venue to match your chosen theme or colour palette.

From elegant and timeless to modern and rustic, their team can help create a setting that complements the natural beauty of the farm.

The farm features beautiful orchid gardens, mature tropical trees, open lawns, and a picturesque water lily pond, providing a variety of stunning locations for your wedding photographs throughout the day.

For couples or guests travelling from out of town, the South Sea Orchids Farmstead offers comfortable on-site accommodation, making it easy to relax before and after the celebration.

Conveniently located just minutes from Nadi International Airport, South Sea Orchids is easily accessible for both local and overseas guests.

With ample parking, flexible venue options, experienced staff, and a beautiful natural setting, everything you need for your special day is in one place.

Contact them today to arrange a viewing and discover how South Sea Orchids can help bring your wedding plans to life.

The venue can accommodate everything from small private ceremonies to celebrations of over 200 guests.

South Sea Orchids Farmstead offers comfortable on-site accommodation, making it easy to relax before and after the celebration. Pictures: SUPPLIED

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: WEDDING BELLS | Your dream wedding, right here in Fiji
item.description: EVERY couple deserves a wedding that reflects their love story, and at Shangri-La Yanuca Island, Fiji, those moments come to life against the breathtaking backdrop of Fiji’s iconic Coral Coast. Whether you’re envisioning an intimate beachfront ceremony, a romantic garden celebration or a grand reception surrounded by family and friends, our dedicated Weddings team works closely with you to bring y
item.content:encoded:
EVERY couple deserves a wedding that reflects their love story, and at Shangri-La Yanuca Island, Fiji, those moments come to life against the breathtaking backdrop of Fiji’s iconic Coral Coast.

Whether you’re envisioning an intimate beachfront ceremony, a romantic garden celebration or a grand reception surrounded by family and friends, our dedicated Weddings team works closely with you to bring your vision to life.

Backed by some of Fiji’s most experienced wedding planners and an exceptional selection of venues, every details thoughtfully tailored to create a celebration that is uniquely yours.

Enjoy the convenience of a fully integrated private island experience, where the seclusion and exclusivity of a 5-star resort are complemented by seamless access to Fiji’s mainland via a private causeway.

From elegant ceremony and reception spaces to luxurious accommodation, exceptional dining, rejuvenating spa experiences, and unforgettable island activities, every element of your wedding celebration is thoughtfully curated within one breathtaking destination.

For couples seeking a seamless, all-in-one celebration, our Tagimocia Deluxe Wedding Package includes a dedicated wedding coordinator, beautifully styled ceremony, traditional Fijian cultural touches, elegant reception, luxurious accommodation, romantic dining and indulgent spa and honeymoon inclusions.

Every detail is thoughtfully curated to create an effortless celebration from the moment you say “I do” to the beginning of your honeymoon.

Our private island setting offers endless opportunities for stunning wedding photography, while authentic cultural touches, including traditional warriors, Meke performances and local cultural experiences, create meaningful memories that will last a lifetime.

The celebration doesn’t have to end after the vows.

Many couples choose to extend the occasion into a weekend getaway with loved ones, enjoying the resort’s restaurants, pools, water sports, golf course, CHI.

The Spa, and the exclusive Adults-Only Reef Wing for aromantic honeymoon without ever leaving the island.

Whether you’re planning months in advance or organising an intimate celebration, our experienced Weddings team is here to guide you every step of the way, making the journey as enjoyable as the day itself.

Begin your forever togetherness beauty, warmth and hospitality of Fiji’s most iconic wedding destination.

Chaple at Shangri.La Yanuca Island Fiji.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Rum Co of Fiji secures global acclaim with White Rum and Spiced Rum of the year titles and a record-breaking 98-points IWSC score
item.description: RUM Co. of Fiji secures global acclaim with White Rum and Spiced Rum of the Year titles and a record-breaking 98-point IWSC score Rum Co. of Fiji has strengthened its position among the world’s leading premium rum producers claiming the coveted titles of White Rum of the Year and Spiced Rum of the Year at the 2026 Meininger’s. International Spirits Award (ISW) in Germany while also achieving its h
item.content:encoded:
RUM Co. of Fiji secures global acclaim with White Rum and Spiced Rum of the Year titles and a record-breaking 98-point IWSC score Rum Co. of Fiji has strengthened its position among the world’s leading premium rum producers claiming the coveted titles of White Rum of the Year and Spiced Rum of the Year at the 2026 Meininger’s.

International Spirits Award (ISW) in Germany while also achieving its highest-ever score at the 2026 International Wine & Spirits Competition (IWSC) in the United Kingdom.

The results further cement Fiji’s growing reputation as a premium rum-producing nation, taking Rum Co. of Fiji’s international medal tally to 313 awards.

At the ISW competition, Rum Co. of Fiji delivered its strongest performance since first entering three years ago.

The awards, judged through a rigorous blind-tasting process by an independent panel of international experts attracted close to 1,300 entries from around the world.

Among the standout achievements, Ratu White Rum was named White Rum of the Year and awarded a Gold Medal while Ratu Spiced Rum secured the title of Spiced Rum of the Year and a Gold Medal.

Additional Gold Medals were awarded to Ratu Dark Rum, Bati Coffee Rum Liqueur and Bati Coconut Rum Liqueur.

The success continued at the IWSC where 10 of Rum Co. of Fiji’s 11 entries received medals.

Leading the results was Ratu 8-Year-Old Signature Rum Liqueur which earned a coveted Gold Outstanding Award with an exceptional score of 98 points, the highest score ever achieved by a Rum Co. of Fiji product at the competition.

2026 Meininger’s International Spirits Award (ISW) – Germany.

Gold – Ratu White Rum, Ratu Spiced Rum – Spiced Rum of the Year, Ratu Dark Rum, Bati Coffee Rum Liqueur, Bati Coconut Rum Liqueur

2026 International Wine & Spirits Competition (IWSC) Gold Outstanding, Ratu 8-Year-Old Signature Rum Liqueur Silver Medals, Ratu 5-Year-Old Dark Rum, Ratu 5-Year-Old Spiced Rum, Bounty Fiji Overproof Dark Rum, Bati Coffee Rum Liqueur.

Bati Banana Rum Liqueur, Bati Coconut Rum Liqueur Bronze Medals, Bati 2-Year-Old Spiced Rum, Ratu 10-Year-Old White Rum, Bounty White Rum – Bronze, Bati 2-Year-Old Dark Rum,

Bati White Rum together the results highlight the depth and versatility of Rum Co. of Fiji’s portfolio with accolades spanning white, spiced, dark and liqueur rum categories across two of the industry’s most respected international competitions.

Crafted from locally grown sugarcane and matured in Fiji’s tropical climate, Rum Co. of Fiji’s products benefit from accelerated interaction between spirit and oak helping create distinctive flavour profiles renowned for their richness, complexity and character.

Combined with meticulous distillation, maturation and blending practices this approach continues to deliver world-class products that stand out on the international stage.

Coca-Cola Europacific Partners Head of Marketing-Alcohol Etrina Simpson said the results represented a significant milestone for both the brand and Fiji’s growing reputation as a producer of premium rum.

“Winning White Rum of the Year and Spiced Rum of the Year at the 2026 Meininger’s International Spirits Award alongside a record 98-point 2026 International Wine & Spirits Competition score for the Ratu 8-Year-Old Signature Rum Liqueur is an exceptional achievement for Rum Co. of Fiji,” said Simpson.

“These awards reflect the quality behind our portfolio while further elevating Fiji’s reputation as a producer of world-class premium rum.

”With 313 international awards now to our name, we’re proud to see Fijian rum continuing to earn recognition among the industry’s most respected global benchmarks.”

As producers and enthusiasts around the world prepare to celebrate World Rum Day on July 11, 2026, the results reinforce Rum Co. of Fiji’s standing in the premium rum category and highlight the growing international appreciation for Fijian rum.

For more details, contact Public Affairs, Communications & Sustainability Specialist, Demayble Pasoni, at demayble.pasoni@ccep.com.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Rehabilitation starts with society
item.description: EVERY prison sentence eventually comes to an end. Superintendent Isireli Dausiga, on the contrary, believes that the real test begins the moment an inmate walks back through the prison gates and into society.
item.content:encoded:
EVERY prison sentence eventually comes to an end.

Superintendent Isireli Dausiga, on the contrary, believes that the real test begins the moment an inmate walks back through the prison gates and into society.

After more than 30 years with the Fiji Corrections Service (FCS), Dausiga has come to believe that rehabilitation is measured not by the time served, but by whether a former offender is accepted by family, embraced by the vanua, and given a genuine opportunity to rebuild a life within the Fijian Scoiety.

As Director of Rehabilitation, Education and Training, he has become one of the driving forces behind a philosophy that places people—not punishment—at the centre of Fiji’s corrections system.

It is a philosophy shaped by decades of frontline experience, but also by lessons learned beyond the prison walls.

A graduate of the 2008 Leadership Fiji program, the Tovu, Totoya, Lau, native credits the experience with broadening his understanding of leadership by exposing him to professionals from industries far removed from the disciplined forces.

“The program allowed me to connect with people from the private sector and other professions,” he said.

Those relationships offered valuable perspectives on solving complex problems and reinforced the importance of partnerships in tackling challenges that no single institution can solve.

That collaborative approach has become the cornerstone of Dausiga’s leadership philosophy.

Having also served in the private sector as Manager of the Wellbeing Unit at the iTaukei Affairs Board, and previously as Chief Officer Operations & Administration for Maximum- and Minimum-Security Prisons at Naboro, he has developed a leadership style that values cooperation across institutions as much as discipline within them.

Dausiga shared that rehabilitation begins by restoring dignity and creating opportunity. While imprisonment removes liberty, he believes it should never remove hope.

“People do change! Time is of the essence,” he said.

Dausiga has contributed alot in the rehabilitation program and the Yellow Ribbon Project since their inception in 2008.

Until today, the FCS has expanded programs that equip inmates with practical skills before they return to society. Partnerships with the Fiji High Education Commission for program and facility accreditation, the Fiji National University provide accredited Class Two Certification to inmates from Medium Corrections Centre for Cabinet Makers & Joiners qualifications, Training of Trainers Level 3 and 2 for officers, while correctional enterprises offer hands-on experience in commercial farming, poultry, piggery and garment manufacturing.

Financial literacy programmes prepare inmates for employment and entrepreneurship, while counselling, behavioral modification and specialized drug rehabilitation seek to address the underlying causes of offending. The above came through the hard work of the Rehab Unit under Dausiga’s command. Collaborations was key!

Yet Dausiga is convinced that no rehabilitation programme can succeed if society refuses to give former offenders a second chance.

“The FCS can prepare an individual for release, but successful reintegration depends on the family, the Vanua and faith-based organizations, employment providers and whole of society,” he said.

This belief has shaped the Yellow Ribbon Project, a reintegration framework built on a “whole-of-society” philosophy that seeks to remove the stigma often faced by former offenders, often labeled as rejects of society or Ex convicts!

Through Community Action for the Rehabilitation of Ex-Offenders (CARE) Network committees established with the (14) provincial councils, reintegration planning begins long before an inmate is released. Traditional leaders, churches and community, non-governmental organizations work alongside the FCS to ensure accommodation, mentorship and local support systems are in place before inmates return home.

The FCS, through the arts exhibitions and employment expos, connect skilled former inmates with prospective employers. Together, these initiatives seek to replace rejection with opportunity.

The results suggest the approach is making a difference.

The FCS reports that under its structured rehabilitation model, Fiji’s national recidivism rate has fallen to just 1.66 per cent. Employment expos have also secured 52 corporate job placements for former offenders, demonstrating the value of involving the private sector in rehabilitation.

Still, Dausiga is careful not to present rehabilitation as a complete success story.

Instead, he views it as an evolving challenge requiring constant adaptation as the crime landscape itself changes dramatically overtime.

One of his greatest concerns is the growing impact of illicit drugs on Fiji’s criminal justice system.

According to Dausiga, about 40 per cent of offences leading to imprisonment are now drug-related, involving trafficking, possession or crimes committed under the influence of illicit substances.

He believes the country’s ageing prison infrastructure, much of it dating back to the colonial era, is no longer suitable for treating addiction as a complex medical and psychological condition.

Rather than simply expanding prison capacity, Dausiga reiterated that the FCS advocated for specialised rehabilitation facilities capable of addressing addiction more effectively. Plans to establish dedicated drug rehabilitation centers in the southern division, alongside the long-term transition from the ageing Suva Prison, reflect FCS conviction that recovery requires purpose-built environments rather than punishment alone.

Reaching young people before prison does

Dausiga is equally concerned by changing crime patterns among Fiji’s youth.

item.title: Fiji-Australia business leaders to connect
item.description: Registrations Now Open Business leaders, investors, government representatives and industry professionals from Fiji, Australia and the wider Pacific are invited to register for the 2026 Fiji-Australia and Australia-Fiji Business Councils Joint Business Forum, taking place at the Grand Pacific Hotel in Suva from 27–29 August 2026. Hosted by the Fiji Australia Business Council (FABC) and the Austral
item.content:encoded:
Registrations Now Open Business leaders, investors, government representatives and industry professionals from Fiji, Australia and the wider Pacific are invited to register for the 2026 Fiji-Australia and Australia-Fiji Business Councils Joint Business Forum, taking place at the Grand Pacific Hotel in Suva from 27–29 August 2026.

Hosted by the Fiji Australia Business Council (FABC) and the Australia Fiji Business Council (AFBC), the flagship three-day event will provide a unique platform for leaders to connect, exchange insights and explore new opportunities for trade, investment and economic cooperation between Fiji and Australia.

Under the theme “Strategy for a Shifting Landscape,” the 2026 Joint Business Forum will focus on how businesses can adapt to changing global and regional conditions, build stronger partnerships and position themselves for future growth.

The Forum programme will feature keynote presentations, expert panel discussions and networking opportunities with senior decision-makers across key sectors including trade, investment, tourism, infrastructure and business development.

Discussions will also explore emerging opportunities linked to regional growth initiatives and the lead-up to the Brisbane 2032 Olympic and Paralympic Games, which is expected to create new opportunities across multiple industries.

“The Joint Business Forum brings together the people and organisations shaping the future of Fiji-Australia commercial relations. It provides an important opportunity for businesses to build connections, gain insights from industry leaders and identify pathways for collaboration and growth,” says FABC president Himen Chandra.

The Forum further strengthens the renewed Fiji–Australia Vuvale Partnership, highlighting the shared commitment of both countries to deepening economic cooperation, promoting investment, and supporting sustainable private sector growth across the Pacific.

President Himen and the Executive members would like to thank all the generous sponsors supporting the 2026 Joint Business Forum; Westpac, Digicel, Swire Shipping, Vision Group and Pleass, whose valued contributions are helping make this important business event possible.

Westpac Fiji, a valued sponsor of the Joint Business Forum, continues to support this important platform bringing together business leaders from Fiji, Australia and the wider Pacific.

“Westpac is proud to continue its support of the Joint Business Forum, which provides a valuable opportunity for leaders to connect, exchange ideas and explore opportunities that strengthen business relationships between Fiji and Australia. We look forward to supporting another successful Forum in 2026,” Westpac Fiji chief executive Shane Smith said.

With limited places available, businesses, investors and professionals are encouraged to secure their participation early. Scan the QR code to view delegate rates, registration packages and register for the 2026 Fiji-Australia and Australia-Fiji Business Councils Joint Business Forum.

Register now and be part of the conversations shaping the future of Fiji-Australia business.

Value City CEO Vera Chute, Ella Mawson of Swire Shipping and FABC executive committee member Glennis Yee at the launch of the FABC-AFBC 2026 joint forum held at the FHL Tower in Suva yesterday. Picture: SUPPLIED

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TOURISM TALANOA | Budget execution
item.description: Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese departed Fiji on Tuesday evening after reaffirming the Vuvale Partnership and discussing economic cooperation between our two countries. Australia has spent years modernising government services, streamlining business approvals and investing heavily in digital public administration. They might not be perfect, but perhaps, while he was here, someone discus
item.content:encoded:
Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese departed Fiji on Tuesday evening after reaffirming the Vuvale Partnership and discussing economic cooperation between our two countries. Australia has spent years modernising government services, streamlining business approvals and investing heavily in digital public administration. They might not be perfect, but perhaps, while he was here, someone discussed sharing how they managed public-sector reform. Australia has been driving major reforms in public sector service delivery through the Australian Public Service (APS) Reform program (2022–2025), anchored by the Public Service Amendment Act 2024. These efforts focus on integrity, efficiency, digital transformation, and stronger community engagement. There may well be a few lessons worth borrowing, especially in the areas of saving, reducing waste and improving productivity, because Vuvale is also about sharing.

This year’s National Budget should have simply said that we’re between a rock and a hard place and everyone needs to work harder with less. Government knows it has little room to spend its way out of its challenges. Revenue is expected to reach around $3.8 billion while expenditure sits at almost $4.9 billion, leaving a deficit of just over $1 billion. More than 80 per cent of spending continues to go towards operating costs rather than capital investment, leaving very little room to introduce major new initiatives.

That changes the conversation. Less about any new spending and more about getting more out of what already exists. Read through the Budget Estimates and the language is remarkably consistent. Efficiency, accountability, improved service delivery, performance budgeting and value for money appear repeatedly. These should have been the strategy a few years ago – not simply supporting principles.

For tourism, that shift is significant because many of the industry’s biggest frustrations have little to do with funding. They stem from delays, duplicated processes and agencies operating in isolation from one another. Businesses can absorb regulation when it is predictable and efficient. What they struggle with is uncertainty, repeated requests for the same information and approval processes that seem to disappear into the system for months.

If Government is asking businesses to become more productive (because everybody knows – when the private sector thrives, the economy grows at full speed), then Government must deliver its services more productively as well. This Budget will ultimately be judged less on what was announced and far more significantly on how it is eventually delivered. The real question is whether businesses will notice the difference in their day-to-day dealings with Government and its many agencies that together are costing $9m a day to run. And probably far more importantly, as we shift into pre-election gear, how the Fijian population will ultimately judge things.

Will approvals for licenses, permits and consents come faster? Will immigration processing improve to enable critical foreign workers to come in to start work on important developments, and who will also be able to get paid when they obtain their Tax Identification Numbers (TIN) and then open their bank accounts? Will businesses spend less time navigating the digital portals that still require hard copies, cash payments and evidence of receipts and consistent phone calls and emails for responses on the system? These are the productivity gains that matter far more than another policy announcement or another online portal.

The Budget points to several areas where Government believes those improvements can be made. Everyone agrees. Border management continues efforts towards more sophisticated use of Advance Passenger Information and Passenger Name Record systems, allowing travellers to be assessed before they even board a flight. In theory, this should create smoother arrivals, reduce congestion at immigration and improve the experience for genuine visitors entering Fiji and will be welcomed.

For a tourism industry welcoming close to one million international visitors annually, those improvements matter. Faster arrivals mean shorter queues, more predictable airport operations and smoother transfers to hotels and resorts. But technology on its own will not achieve that outcome. The information may arrive earlier, but unless staffing, procedures and decision-making also change, the bottleneck simply moves further down the process. Somebody still needs to turn the machines on, ensure the data is being captured, check scanners are working, open the doors and reset the Wi-Fi to ensure it’s working.

Digitising a slow process does not automatically make it a faster one. If applications still pass through the same number of desks, require the same approvals and wait for the same signatories, businesses simply end up waiting electronically instead of waiting on paper. The queue becomes easier to see, but it does not become any shorter. And if we are adopting better technology across all aspects of government – doesn’t this therefore mean that we will require fewer processes, fewer people in some instances and faster responses?

Online applications may improve visibility, but without clear service standards, delegated authority for straightforward applications and realistic turnaround times where agencies are held accountable – we’re simply spinning doing the same thing while expecting a different result. But we are trying to be positive, especially as we’re being asked to provide recommendations to the Liquor Act, and this is a welcome sign, among others, that some areas in government are trying harder than others.

Transparency is another major theme running through the Budget. Recent public discussion around the national eTransport card system is an interesting example because the debate was never really about bus fares. It was about whether digital systems actually improve accountability by making transactions visible, traceable and measurable. Most do not believe it does any of these things, and the ones who disagree probably do not catch a bus anyway, so their opinion shouldn’t matter.

The same thinking should now apply across Government. Businesses should be able to see where an application is sitting, which agency is responsible for the next step and how long each stage is expected to take. Transparency should not simply expose delays. It should become the mechanism that drives improvement, with accountability providing the right emphasis for either kicking the right butts or rolling the right heads.

The challenge for tourism is that all of these reforms are arriving at the same time. Operators are already preparing for changes associated with Tourism Services Tax (TST), the Commercial Use of Marine Areas (CUMA) legislation, the rollout of the VAT Monitoring System (VMS) and the Tourism Bill. Each initiative may have a legitimate policy objective. Collectively, however, they create a growing compliance burden that businesses are expected to absorb, work through and become compliant with.

Too often, the tourism industry is invited into the conversation after the policy has largely been designed. Consultation becomes a discussion about managing consequences instead of shaping practical solutions from the beginning. That approach creates unnecessary friction and often results in legislation that requires further refinement once implementation begins, thus creating more work and headaches.

Perhaps the biggest challenge emerging from this Budget is that individually, almost every initiative makes some sense. Collectively, they risk creating a system that becomes increasingly difficult to navigate. Tourism businesses already work with immigration, FRCS, municipal councils, biosecurity, Investment Fiji, planning authorities, environmental regulators and several Government ministries. Every agency has an important role. The issue is whether those agencies are working as One Government from the perspective of the businesses they regulate.

The Budget speaks repeatedly about productivity, performance and accountability. Repeatedly. These objectives are supported because we depend on efficient government services just as much as efficient private businesses. But productivity is not achieved by asking operators to complete more forms through more digital portals. It comes from removing duplication, simplifying approvals and allowing agencies to share information rather than requiring businesses to submit the same information over and over again.

So ultimately this looks more like a management budget. Government has effectively acknowledged that it cannot spend its way towards better public services. It must manage its way there, and that requires coordination, discipline and execution across the public sector.

We support that direction. However, ambition alone will not shorten immigration queues, reduce liquor licensing delays, simplify VMS implementation or ensure that CUMA, the Tourism Bill and other reforms fit together in a way that supports investment rather than complicates it. Those outcomes depend on something much less glamorous than national budget announcements. They depend on departments talking to each other before businesses are forced to.

That is where Fiji’s next productivity gains will be found, and where this budget will ultimately succeed or fail.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Dry eyes on the rise | What you need to know
item.description: Dry, irritated eyes are becoming an increasingly common complaint among people in Fiji. From office workers staring at computer screens to farmers working long hours under the tropical sun, dry eye disease is affecting individuals across all age groups. While often dismissed as a minor discomfort, dry eyes can significantly affect vision, work productivity, and overall quality of life if left untr
item.content:encoded:
Dry, irritated eyes are becoming an increasingly common complaint among people in Fiji. From office workers staring at computer screens to farmers working long hours under the tropical sun, dry eye disease is affecting individuals across all age groups. While often dismissed as a minor discomfort, dry eyes can significantly affect vision, work productivity, and overall quality of life if left untreated.

Dry eye disease occurs when the eyes do not produce enough tears, or when the tears evaporate too quickly. Tears are essential for keeping the eyes comfortable, clear, and protected from infection. In Fiji’s warm, windy, and often air-conditioned environments, the natural tear film can be disrupted more easily, leading to dryness and irritation.

Common symptoms include burning or stinging sensations, redness, gritty or sandy feeling in the eyes, blurred or fluctuating vision, sensitivity to light, and paradoxically, excessive watering of the eyes.

Understanding the tear film and pathophysiology

The tear film has three layers: an oily layer, a watery layer, and a mucus layer. Together, they keep the eye surface smooth and moist. Dry eye disease develops when one or more of these layers is affected.

The central mechanism involves tear film instability and increased tear saltiness, known as hyperosmolarity. This triggers inflammation of the eye surface, damaging cells that produce protective mucus and worsening dryness. Over time, this creates a vicious cycle of irritation, inflammation, and tear film breakdown.

Dry eye disease is broadly divided into two main types, though many patients have features of both.

Aqueous deficient dry eye occurs when the lacrimal glands fail to produce enough tears. This is more common in older individuals and in those with autoimmune diseases.

Evaporative dry eye is the most common form seen in Fiji. It occurs when tears evaporate too quickly, often due to dysfunction of the meibomian glands in the eyelids. These glands produce oil that slows tear evaporation. Blocked or inflamed glands, often worsened by dust, smoke, and prolonged screen use, contribute significantly to this condition.

Several systemic and environmental factors common in Fiji increase the risk of dry eyes:

• Diabetes mellitus, which is prevalent in Fiji, can damage corneal nerves and reduce tear production.

• Autoimmune conditions , such as rheumatoid arthritis and Sjögren’s syndrome, can severely affect tear glands.

• Hormonal changes, particularly in postmenopausal women.

• Medications , including antihistamines, blood pressure drugs, and antidepressants.

• Climate and environment, including heat, trade winds, dust, and prolonged exposure to sunlight.

• Digital eye strain, with increasing use of mobile phones and computers.

• Air-conditioned offices and vehicles, which dry the surrounding air.

Dry eye disease is manageable, especially when diagnosed early. Treatment is tailored based on severity and cause.

For mild cases, lifestyle changes such as taking regular screen breaks, conscious blinking, wearing sunglasses outdoors, and staying well hydrated can help.

Artificial tears, widely available in pharmacies across Fiji, are often the first line of treatment. Preservative-free drops are preferred for frequent use.

When inflammation is present, prescription eye drops, including short courses of anti-inflammatory medications, may be required under medical supervision.

For patients with eyelid-related problems, warm compresses and lid hygiene play a crucial role. In some cases, oral medications may be prescribed to improve oil gland function.

More advanced options include punctal plugs to reduce tear drainage and specialised eye drops, such as serum-based treatments, available through specialist care.

Persistent eye discomfort, redness, or blurred vision should not be ignored. Early consultation with an optometrist or ophthalmologist can prevent complications and improve long-term eye health.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
